title: Sada roti
subtitle: Trinidadian plain flatbread
excerpt: A simple, leavened flatbread made from plain flour and baking powder, cooked on a dry tawa until blistered and puffed. Sada roti is the everyday bread of Trinidad, quick to make, requiring no filling or rolling skill, and eaten at breakfast with bhaji (spinach), choka, or baigan choka. The inside should be soft and slightly doughy with a dry, blistered exterior.
sourceNotes: Sada roti comes from the Indian culinary tradition brought to Trinidad by indentured workers. 'Sada' means plain in Hindi. It is the base roti of Trinidadian cooking, the starting point before more complex versions like dhalpuri and buss-up-shut. It appears as the everyday breakfast bread across all communities in Trinidad and in the Trinidadian diaspora worldwide.

content.text: Sada roti dough should be soft, softer than bread dough, about the consistency of a smooth earlobe. If the dough feels stiff, it will produce a tough roti. The resting time allows the gluten to relax and makes rolling much easier.

Cook on a dry tawa or heavy flat pan over medium-high heat. The first side needs about 45 seconds before flipping; the roti should have dry patches and the beginning of blistering. After flipping, use a folded cloth to press the roti down and encourage even contact with the pan. It is done when it puffs and has golden-brown blisters on both sides.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add the {{vegetable-oil}} and rub into the flour. Add warm water a little at a time (about 160 ml total) until the dough comes together into a soft, slightly sticky ball. Knead for 3 minutes until smooth. Cover and rest for 15 minutes. Divide into 4 balls. On a lightly floured surface, roll each ball into a round about 22 cm across and 3 mm thick. Cook on a preheated dry tawa or heavy pan over medium-high heat for 45 seconds to 1 minute per side, pressing with a cloth, until blistered and puffed. Stack and cover immediately to keep soft.

items.fix: Add more water to the dough. The inside should remain soft, cook only until blistered, not until crisp
items.cause: Dough too stiff or cooked too long

items.fix: The pan needs to be fully preheated, a drop of water should evaporate immediately on contact. Roll to no more than 3 mm
items.cause: Pan not hot enough or dough rolled too thick

items.fix: A properly preheated tawa does not need oiling for sada roti. Brush off excess flour before placing the roti in the pan
items.cause: Pan not hot enough or too much flour on the surface

content.text: Sada roti is the everyday bread of Trinidad, made for breakfast, eaten with spinach bhaji, tomato choka, baigan choka, or eggs. It is the foundation that dhalpuri and buss-up-shut are built upon, and the simplest version of the Trinidadian roti tradition. In homes throughout Trinidad and the diaspora, the tawa is a permanent fixture on the stove for morning roti-making. The whole process from flour to table is under 30 minutes.
